policy_statement: Double the City’s budget allocation from 2021–2025 and target an overall 40% energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
mechanism_description: Increase funding to retrofit/upgrade municipal buildings, reducing building energy consumption and associated carbon emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: "achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025"
primary_causal_pathway: Building systems upgrade → ↓ energy demand (electricity + fuels) → ↓ combustion/generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human/system change: Retrofit existing buildings by improving envelopes (insulation, windows, air sealing), upgrading HVAC and water heating, adding controls/commissioning, and/or installing efficient lighting and appliances.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced heat loss/gain and improved equipment efficiency/control reduce delivered energy required for heating, cooling, hot water, and plug/lighting loads; in many cases electrification shifts remaining energy away from direct combustion.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower on-site fossil fuel use and reduced electricity demand (and associated generation) decrease CO₂e emissions; secondary co-effects can include peak load reduction and improved indoor comfort.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption, which lowers upstream and on-site GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Building retrofits primarily act by reducing energy use, which reduces GHG emissions from fuel combustion and electricity generation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although retrofits can enhance resilience (e.g., better thermal comfort during heat waves) and improve resource efficiency in a general sense, the main climate-relevant effect consistently targeted and measured is emissions reduction via lower energy demand—therefore Mitigation is primary.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “retrofit” is undertaken solely for hazard hardening (e.g., floodproofing) without material energy impacts, it would shift toward Adaptation, but that is outside this canonical mechanism (“energy_use_reduction”). If retrofits include onsite renewables, that is a different/additional mechanism; here the core effect is demand reduction.  
4) Confirm applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism (retrofit → reduced energy use) necessarily produces mitigation as the dominant climate effect because reduced energy demand reduces emissions intensity-weighted consumption, regardless of city/context.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., envelope vs HVAC vs controls, electrification vs efficiency-only, equity-targeted programs) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some retrofit programs bundle electrification, fuel-switching, demand response, or resilience upgrades; if the primary intent/effect shifts away from energy reduction (or includes substantial renewables generation), classification may require separating mechanisms. Otherwise, straightforward.
key_indicators: “double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025”; “set a new target”; “achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction”; “in municipal buildings by 2025”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits include improved indoor comfort and air quality, reduced operating costs, and reduced peak demand (not explicitly stated in the text).
instance_edge_case_notes: Budget language could also be read as an enabling funding commitment (incentive-program/infrastructure-investment), but no specific funded projects or grant/rebate structure are described; the clearest instrument signal is the quantified reduction target with a deadline.
